title: Erd\H{o}s Unit Distance Problem and Graph Rigidity
speaker: Orit Raz
abstract:
Erd\H{o}s unit distance problem asks the following: Let P
 be a set of n distinct points in the plane, and let U(P)
 denote the number of pairs of points in P that are at distance 1. How large can U(P) be? In 1946, Erd\H{o}s showed that for P=[n‾√]×[n‾√], one has U(P)=Θ(n1+cloglogn), for some constant c greater than 0, and he conjectured that this is best possible. While the problem has received a lot of attention, the best upper bound, established by Spencer, Szemer\'edi, and Trotter in 1984, is U(P)=O(n4/3), with no progress in the last 40 years. One reason for the difficulty of the problem is that the current combinatorial geometry tools, dealing with properties of unit circles, actually apply to more general families of curves. For the latter families, the upper bound of O(n4/3) is, in fact, tight. 
In the talk, I will introduce a completely new approach to the unit distance problem, relating the problem to a question in graph rigidity theory. Specifically, I will present a new structure theorem, that applies to point configurations with many unit distances, and pose a new conjecture regarding rigid subgraphs. I will then explain how a solution of the rigidity conjecture, would, for the first time, yield an improvement of the aforementioned upper bound for the unit distance problem. If time permits, I will explain how to prove a weaker version of the rigidity conjecture, by reducing the problem to a line-line incidence question in ℝ3.
The talk is based on a joint work with J. Pach and J. Solymosi.

proof_stages.label: Unit-distance problem and benchmark bounds
proof_stages.summary: The talk defines U(P) and U(n), recalls Erdős's lattice lower bound and conjectural near-linear behavior, and reviews that all known upper-bound approaches still give the n^{4/3} barrier.

proof_stages.label: Incidence reformulation and barrier diagnosis
proof_stages.summary: Unit distances are recast as incidences between points and unit circles, and comparison with tight point-line incidence bounds shows that any improvement must exploit special properties of unit circles rather than generic incidence technology.

proof_stages.label: Framework rigidity for specific embeddings
proof_stages.summary: The speaker introduces rigid frameworks, distinguishes local rigidity of a given embedding from generic graph rigidity, and emphasizes that the unit-distance application involves highly nongeneric configurations.

proof_stages.label: Contradiction strategy via repeated non-unit distances
proof_stages.summary: Assuming a point set has more than n^{4/3}/log n unit distances, the proposed strategy is to find a structured subset where rigidity forces many repeated distances of some value other than one, yielding a contradiction.

proof_stages.label: Many common-length embeddings of a rigid graph
proof_stages.summary: The incidence decomposition is described as producing a subset and many embeddings of a generically rigid graph H with the same unit edge lengths and distinct marked vertex pairs, which would force repeated marked distances if the actual embeddings were rigid.

proof_stages.label: Dense embedded-graph rigid-subframework problem
proof_stages.summary: The remaining obstacle is formulated as a rigidity-theoretic theorem/conjecture: a dense embedded graph satisfying weak nondegeneracy should contain a rigid subframework, proved in the work for alpha at least 1/2 and conjecturally needed at alpha=1/6 for the Erdős application.

proof_stages.label: Incidence tools and parameter losses
proof_stages.summary: In response to questions, the speaker explains that their rigidity problem is reduced to a line-line incidence problem in R^3 and that logarithmic losses arise from repeated pigeonholing over graphs, subgraphs, and finitely many noncongruent embeddings.
